metadata.notes: Format considered healthy per March 2026 B&R announcement. No changes. Energy cards from Modern Horizons 3 have created viable non-blue aggro strategies. Dimir Tempo is the clear best deck but not oppressively so.

decks.name: Dimir Tempo
decks.description: The format's premier tempo deck, leveraging the power of Orcish Bowmasters, efficient black disruption, and blue card selection. Combines Brainstorm/Ponder with Thoughtseize and cheap threats like Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student and Murktide Regent to apply pressure while denying the opponent resources. The most played deck in Legacy by a wide margin.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Brainstorm/Ponder/Force of Will/Daze shell with Izzet Delver but trades red removal for black disruption. Sultai Tempo adds green for Uro. Death's Shadow is a more aggressive variant of the same UB core.
decks.change_notes: Card choices flex between Fatal Push, Snuff Out, and Dismember based on metagame. Sideboard heavily tuned week to week. Barrowgoyf from MH3 was a major addition in 2024.

key_cards_explained.Orcish Bowmasters: Premier creature in the format. Punishes Brainstorm, provides removal, and creates a growing threat. Core to why black is the best color in Legacy.
key_cards_explained.Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student: Efficient one-drop that flips into a planeswalker generating card advantage. Replaced Delver of Secrets in many UB lists.
key_cards_explained.Murktide Regent: Large delve threat that comes down for UU and dominates combat. Fueled by the many instants and sorceries in the deck.
key_cards_explained.Barrowgoyf: MH3 Tarmogoyf variant that is even more efficient in a format full of diverse card types in graveyards.
key_cards_explained.Daze: Free countermagic that punishes opponents for tapping out early, enabling the tempo gameplan.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Removed the Izzet version's best card advantage engine, helping push players toward Dimir for Bowmasters.

historical_timeline.event: Deck emerges after Orcish Bowmasters printed in Lord of the Rings (June). Black overtakes red as the partner color for blue tempo shells. Early UB Scam lists use Grief for free disruption.
MH3 adds Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student and Barrowgoyf, cementing UB as the best Delver/tempo shell. Tamiyo replaces Delver of Secrets. Deck overtakes Izzet Delver in metagame share.
key_changes: Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student replaces Delver of Secrets

historical_timeline.event: Entomb ban removes Reanimator from tier 1, and Dimir Tempo benefits from less combo in the meta. Becomes the most-played deck in Legacy by a wide margin.
key_changes: Murktide Regent as top-end finisher solidified

historical_timeline.event: Continues as the format's best deck at 14.6% meta share. No changes in March B&R. Lists are highly optimized with small flex slot variations week to week.
key_changes: Flex between Fatal Push, Snuff Out, Spell Pierce counts

decks.name: Ocelot Pride Midrange
decks.description: An energy-based midrange deck built around Ocelot Pride, Guide of Souls, and Amped Raptor. Represents the most successful non-blue aggressive strategy in Legacy, using efficient energy creatures and Swords to Plowshares for interaction. The deck pressures blue combo and control while going over the top of other creature decks with token generation.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the green creature toolbox approach of Maverick but eschews the Knight of the Reliquary package for energy threats. Uses a similar Green Sun's Zenith shell.
decks.change_notes: Relatively new archetype still being optimized. Naya vs Selesnya builds are both viable. Amped Raptor cascade hits vary by build.

key_cards_explained.Ocelot Pride: One-mana 1/1 with lifelink that creates cat tokens whenever you gain life and deal combat damage. Generates massive board presence.
key_cards_explained.Guide of Souls: Energy-generating creature that makes tokens fly temporarily, enabling evasive damage for Ocelot Pride triggers.
key_cards_explained.Amped Raptor: Energy creature with cascade for MV equal to energy spent, providing card advantage in a creature shell.
key_cards_explained.Galvanic Discharge: Energy-based removal spell that scales up, handling even large threats like Murktide Regent.

historical_timeline.event: Deck created after MH3 prints Ocelot Pride, Guide of Souls, and Amped Raptor. Energy package gives non-blue decks a competitive creature suite. Naya builds with Green Sun's Zenith emerge.
key_changes: Guide of Souls for energy and evasion

historical_timeline.event: Deck rises to tier 1 as pilots optimize the mana base and sideboard. Proves it can compete with blue decks through raw board presence and Swords to Plowshares.
key_changes: Urza's Saga added for artifact tutoring
Endurance as green pitch answer to graveyards

historical_timeline.event: Second most-played deck at 8.5%. Favorable matchup against Dimir Tempo keeps it popular. The best non-blue deck in Legacy.
key_changes: Ajani, Nacatl Pariah as additional threat

decks.name: Oops! All Spells
decks.description: A blazing-fast combo deck that wins by milling its entire library using Balustrade Spy or Undercity Informer (the deck plays zero lands, using only mana-producing artifacts and MDFCs). Once the library is milled, it uses Narcomoeba triggers and Dread Return to reanimate a win condition. Among the fastest combo decks in the format.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the all-in graveyard combo approach with LED Dredge but is generally faster and more fragile. Both use free countermagic from the sideboard.
decks.change_notes: Core combo is static. Flex slots are the protection suite (Pact of Negation vs Force of Will main) and the exact MDFC package. LothlÃ³rien Adventurer from LTR was a significant upgrade.

key_cards_explained.Balustrade Spy: Mills your entire library since you play zero actual lands. This enables the whole combo.
key_cards_explained.Undercity Informer: Redundant copy of the Spy effect, ensuring consistency.
key_cards_explained.Narcomoeba: Free creatures from milling that enable Dread Return.
key_cards_explained.Dread Return: Flashback reanimates Thassa's Oracle for the win.
key_cards_explained.LothlÃ³rien Adventurer: MDFC that counts as a spell for the combo but taps for mana as a land.

historical_timeline.event: Deck first appears after Gatecrash prints Balustrade Spy and Undercity Informer. The zero-land combo using Narcomoeba and Dread Return is discovered.
key_changes: Balustrade Spy + Undercity Informer as combo enablers
Narcomoeba + Dread Return for win condition
Dark Ritual, Chrome Mox, Lotus Petal for fast mana

historical_timeline.event: Deck remains a fringe but explosive combo option. Consistency issues keep it from tier 1. Occasionally spikes tournaments.
key_changes: Shield Sphere as free creature for Informer

historical_timeline.event: Zendikar Rising MDFCs (Agadeem's Awakening, Sea Gate Restoration, etc.) massively boost consistency. Spell-lands that count as spells for the combo but provide mana. Deck jumps in viability.
Continued refinement with MDFCs. Thassa's Oracle replaces older win conditions as a cleaner finish.
key_changes: Thassa's Oracle as primary win condition

historical_timeline.event: LotR set adds Lothlorien Adventurer, another useful spell-creature. Deck continues to perform as a fast combo option.
key_changes: Refined mana base with new MDFCs

historical_timeline.event: Entomb ban devastates Reanimator, and combo players migrate to Oops as the premier fast combo deck. Meta share spikes significantly.
key_changes: Becomes top combo deck in the format

historical_timeline.event: Third most-played deck at 8.2% meta share. The fastest goldfish in the format keeps it popular despite vulnerability to graveyard hate.
key_changes: Chrome Mox and Mox Opal as acceleration

decks.name: Sneak and Show
decks.description: A classic Legacy combo deck that cheats Emrakul, the Aeons Torn or Atraxa, Grand Unifier into play via Show and Tell or Sneak Attack. Uses blue cantrips and countermagic to find and protect the combo. One of the most enduring archetypes in Legacy history.
decks.similarity_notes: Omni-Tell shares Show and Tell but uses Omniscience instead of Sneak Attack. Both are blue combo decks using the same cantrip and counter shell.
decks.change_notes: Extremely stable archetype. Main innovation was adding Atraxa as a Show and Tell target alongside Emrakul. Sol land counts flex based on metagame speed.

key_cards_explained.Show and Tell: Puts Emrakul or Atraxa directly into play for just 2U, bypassing their enormous mana costs.
key_cards_explained.Sneak Attack: Gives haste to cheated creatures, enabling immediate attacks with Emrakul's annihilator and extra turn triggers.
key_cards_explained.Emrakul, the Aeons Torn: The premier win condition. Annihilator 6 and an extra turn usually ends the game in one attack.
key_cards_explained.Atraxa, Grand Unifier: Backup threat that draws 7+ cards off Show and Tell, burying the opponent in card advantage even if they have an answer.

historical_timeline.event: Deck solidifies as a Legacy archetype using Sneak Attack and Show and Tell to cheat Emrakul, the Aeons Torn and Griselbrand into play. Blue shell provides Force of Will protection.
key_changes: Sneak Attack + Show and Tell as cheat engines
Emrakul, the Aeons Torn as primary threat
Force of Will, Brainstorm, Ponder shell

historical_timeline.event: Griselbrand printed in Avacyn Restored gives the deck its best Show and Tell target. Drawing 7 cards makes the combo almost unbeatable when it resolves.
key_changes: Griselbrand added as premier Show and Tell target

historical_timeline.event: Remains a top combo deck through multiple metagame shifts. Very stable core means bans rarely affect it directly.
Benefits somewhat from Sensei's Divining Top ban gutting Miracles, as Counterbalance was a strong answer to Show and Tell. Continues as tier 1 combo.
key_changes: Miracles (a tough matchup) weakened by Top ban

historical_timeline.event: MH2 Delver threats make tempo matchups harder. Deck endures but faces more efficient counterplay.
key_changes: Faces tougher Delver matchups with Murktide/Ragavan

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters doesn't directly affect the deck. Remains a consistent tier 2 combo strategy that punishes slow blue decks.
key_changes: Atraxa, Grand Unifier as alternative fatty

historical_timeline.event: Entomb ban removes Reanimator competition. Sneak and Show rises as players want a cheat-into-play strategy.
Fourth most-played deck at 6.7%. The most stable combo archetype in Legacy's history, with a nearly unchanged core since 2012.
key_changes: Occasional Show and Tell target updates

decks.name: Izzet Delver
decks.description: The original Delver tempo shell using Delver of Secrets alongside Lightning Bolt and red sideboard cards. Once the dominant deck in Legacy, it has lost ground to Dimir Tempo since Orcish Bowmasters was printed, but remains competitive with its unique strengths: direct damage reach and superior red removal.
decks.similarity_notes: The original template from which Dimir Tempo diverged. Shares the core Brainstorm/Ponder/Force/Daze engine but uses red for removal over black for disruption.
decks.change_notes: Lost Expressive Iteration to a ban in 2023. Dragon's Rage Channeler and Murktide Regent were major additions from MH2. Still searching for the right threat mix post-Bowmasters.

key_cards_explained.Delver of Secrets: The namesake one-drop that flips into a 3/2 flyer. With 30+ instants/sorceries, it flips reliably.
key_cards_explained.Lightning Bolt: The key advantage over Dimir: efficient removal that also goes face, providing reach to close games.
key_cards_explained.Dragon's Rage Channeler: Surveil on cast fuels delirium for a 3/3 flyer and fills the graveyard for Murktide Regent.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost the deck's best card advantage engine. Contributed to the shift from UR to UB.
Removed a key threat that gave the deck recurring card advantage.

historical_timeline.event: Delver of Secrets printed in Innistrad. RUG Delver (Canadian Threshold) immediately becomes the best tempo deck, using Delver, Nimble Mongoose, Tarmogoyf with Daze, Force, Wasteland, and Stifle.
key_changes: Delver of Secrets as transformative one-drop
Core: Daze, Force of Will, Wasteland, Stifle, Lightning Bolt
Nimble Mongoose and Tarmogoyf as threats

historical_timeline.event: RUG Delver and Miracles define the format. RUG is the premier tempo strategy. Snapcaster Mage adopted in some builds.
key_changes: Format revolves around RUG vs Miracles

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman pushes BUG Delver ahead of RUG. True-Name Nemesis (November) gives blue tempo an uncounterable threat. Some RUG pilots switch to BUG.
key_changes: BUG Delver overtakes RUG due to Deathrite Shaman

historical_timeline.event: BUG/Sultai Delver with Deathrite Shaman dominates the tempo slot. RUG Delver still viable but clearly second best.
key_changes: Lightning Bolt remains key advantage over BUG

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman BANNED (July). RUG Delver immediately returns to prominence. Gitaxian Probe ban also impacts tempo strategies.
key_changes: DRS ban brings players back to RUG

historical_timeline.event: Wrenn and Six from MH1 makes RUG Delver oppressive — recurring Wasteland is devastating. Wrenn and Six BANNED in November.
MH2 transforms the deck: Dragon's Rage Channeler, Murktide Regent, and Ragavan, Nimble Pilferer replace the old threats. UR Delver becomes the clear best deck, dropping green entirely.
key_changes: Dragon's Rage Channeler replaces Nimble Mongoose
Deck shifts from RUG to UR

historical_timeline.event: UR Delver with Ragavan dominates Legacy. Expressive Iteration provides exceptional card advantage. Deck is ~15% of the meta and the best deck.
key_changes: Expressive Iteration as card advantage engine

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters punishes cantrips and small creatures, weakening Delver mirrors. Expressive Iteration banned (March). Many players switch to Dimir Tempo for Bowmasters. UR Delver drops.
key_changes: Many pilots migrate to Dimir Tempo

historical_timeline.event: Remains a solid tier 2 option at 4.3%. UR shell with Delver, DRC, Murktide, and Lightning Bolt for players who prefer red over black.
key_changes: Plays Delver where Dimir plays Tamiyo

decks.name: Painter
decks.description: A mono-red combo deck that wins by combining Painter's Servant with Grindstone to mill the opponent's entire library in one activation. Backed by blood moon effects and prison elements to slow opponents while assembling the combo. Urza's Saga provides a tutor for Grindstone.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Ancient Tomb / Blood Moon prison plan with Red Stompy, but adds the Painter-Grindstone combo as an instant-win condition.
decks.change_notes: Urza's Saga was a major upgrade, providing a tutor for Grindstone. Goblin Engineer replaced some older tutor options. Fable of the Mirror-Breaker added as value/filtering.

key_cards_explained.Painter's Servant: Names blue, making all cards in the opponent's library blue, which enables Grindstone to mill the entire library at once.
key_cards_explained.Grindstone: With Painter's Servant in play, mills the entire library since every pair of cards shares a color.
key_cards_explained.Goblin Welder: Recurs Grindstone from the graveyard and enables artifact synergies.
key_cards_explained.Red Elemental Blast: With Painter naming blue, this counters or destroys any permanent, acting as one-mana universal removal.

historical_timeline.event: Painter's Servant + Grindstone combo deck first appears in Legacy. Uses Imperial Recruiter to find Painter and fast mana from Ancient Tomb.
Deck maintains fringe presence. Red Elemental Blast becomes a hard counter against everything with Painter's Servant naming blue.
key_changes: Red Elemental Blast as universal counter with Painter

historical_timeline.event: Top ban opens space for non-Miracles combo-control decks. Painter remains a consistent fringe option.
key_changes: Less Counterbalance in the format benefits Painter

historical_timeline.event: Urza's Saga from MH2 is transformative — tutors for Grindstone, creates tokens, and provides an alternative win condition. Deck jumps in power level.
Fable of the Mirror-Breaker experimented with. Deck remains solid tier 2 with Urza's Saga giving it staying power.
Stable at 4.1% meta share as a tier 2 combo-stompy deck. Urza's Saga and the Painter combo give it redundancy few other decks have.

decks.name: Lands
decks.description: A land-based control deck that uses Life from the Loam, Wasteland recursion, and Maze of Ith to control the board while assembling the Dark Depths + Thespian's Stage combo to create a 20/20 Marit Lage token. One of the most unique decks in Legacy, barely playing any spells.
decks.similarity_notes: Selesnya Depths shares the Dark Depths combo but in a creature-based shell. Lands is more controlling and land-focused.
decks.change_notes: Very stable core. Elvish Reclaimer was the most significant addition in recent years. Urza's Saga provides additional utility and Construct tokens. Field of the Dead is a newer addition for grindy games.

key_cards_explained.Dark Depths: Combines with Thespian's Stage to instantly create a 20/20 flying indestructible Marit Lage token.
key_cards_explained.Thespian's Stage: Copies Dark Depths, and since it enters with no ice counters, immediately creates Marit Lage.
key_cards_explained.Life from the Loam: Engine card that recurs Wasteland, cycling lands, and combo pieces from the graveyard.
key_cards_explained.The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale: Forces opponents to pay for their creatures each turn or sacrifice them. Devastating against creature decks.
key_cards_explained.Maze of Ith: Free repeatable removal that negates any single attacker.

historical_timeline.event: Lands establishes itself as a Legacy archetype using Life from the Loam, Wasteland lock, Maze of Ith, and The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale to control the board. Punishing Fire + Grove of the Burnwillows provides creature removal.
key_changes: Life from the Loam + Wasteland recursion
Maze of Ith and Tabernacle for creature control
Punishing Fire + Grove of the Burnwillows

historical_timeline.event: Dark Depths + Thespian's Stage combo becomes a core part of the deck, giving it a 20/20 indestructible flying win condition alongside the Loam grind plan.
key_changes: Dark Depths + Thespian's Stage as primary win condition

historical_timeline.event: DRS ban helps Lands significantly — Deathrite Shaman was excellent graveyard hate against the Loam engine. Format becomes more favorable.
key_changes: Deathrite Shaman ban removes key hate piece
Field of the Dead experimented with later

historical_timeline.event: Urza's Saga from MH2 adds a powerful threat and tutor to the deck. Can find Expedition Map or Pithing Needle while making Construct tokens.
key_changes: Urza's Saga as threat and tutor
Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth enables easier Depths combo

historical_timeline.event: Minsc & Boo or Orcish Bowmasters in some RG or BG Lands builds. Deck continues as a consistent tier 2 option that punishes greedy mana bases.
key_changes: Boseiju, Who Endures as uncounterable removal

historical_timeline.event: Steady at 3.8% meta share. Favorable matchup against Dimir Tempo keeps it relevant. The Wasteland + Loam lock remains one of Legacy's most punishing strategies.
key_changes: Endurance in sideboard for graveyard matchups

decks.name: The EPIC Storm
decks.description: A fast storm combo deck that uses mana acceleration and tutors to generate a lethal storm count, typically winning with Tendrils of Agony or burning through the deck with Ad Nauseam. Faster and more aggressive than ANT, trading consistency for speed with Chrome Mox and Rite of Flame.
decks.similarity_notes: Both are UBx storm-adjacent combo decks, but TES wins through traditional storm count while Doomsday uses a specific five-card pile.
decks.change_notes: Constant tuning by its dedicated community. Galvanic Relay and Beseech the Mirror are relatively recent additions that replaced older cards like Infernal Tutor. The deck evolves significantly with each new set.

key_cards_explained.Ad Nauseam: Draws cards equal to your life total (effectively), providing enough gas to generate lethal storm count in one big turn.
key_cards_explained.Lion's Eye Diamond: The deck's Black Lotus equivalent. Discarding your hand is mitigated by LED in response to Burning Wish or Brainstorm.
key_cards_explained.Burning Wish: Tutors for Tendrils of Agony, Empty the Warrens, or silver bullets from the sideboard, providing versatility.
key_cards_explained.Galvanic Relay: Storm-count payoff that exiles cards for next turn, providing a resilient way to continue comboing through disruption.

historical_timeline.event: TES evolves from older Legacy Storm lists, focusing on speed over resilience compared to Ad Nauseam Tendrils (ANT). Uses Burning Wish, Rite of Flame, and Lion's Eye Diamond.
key_changes: Rite of Flame, Dark Ritual, Lion's Eye Diamond for mana
Infernal Tutor, Ad Nauseam as engines

historical_timeline.event: Mental Misstep ban is positive for Storm — Misstep was devastating against Dark Ritual and Rite of Flame. Deck improves.
key_changes: Mental Misstep ban removes key hate piece

historical_timeline.event: Treasure Cruise / Dig Through Time ban is neutral. Storm remains a consistent combo option. Gitaxian Probe used for information.
Gitaxian Probe BANNED (July). Storm loses its best free cantrip and information spell. Deck adapts but takes a hit.
Continues adapting. Galvanic Relay from MH2 provides a new engine for grinding through disruption. Deck gets new tools.
key_changes: Deck pivots between speed and resilience builds

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters is irrelevant to TES since it doesn't play creatures. Storm exists in a meta where creature decks draw the hate.
Stable tier 2 at 3.5% meta share. One of Legacy's most enduring archetypes with a dedicated player base that continuously optimizes.

decks.name: Eldrazi
decks.description: A colorless aggro-prison deck using sol lands and Eldrazi creatures to deploy oversized threats ahead of curve while disrupting opponents with Chalice of the Void and Thought-Knot Seer. Preys on cantrip-heavy decks with Chalice on 1.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the sol land + Chalice prison plan with Red Stompy but uses colorless Eldrazi instead of red threats. Both attack from a similar angle.
decks.change_notes: Very stable core since 2016. Urza's Saga was the biggest recent addition, providing Construct tokens and artifact tutoring.

key_cards_explained.Chalice of the Void: On X=1, shuts down Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords, Bolt, and most other one-mana staples in the format.
key_cards_explained.Thought-Knot Seer: Disrupts the opponent's hand while providing a 4/4 body. The exile effect dodges graveyard synergies.
key_cards_explained.Eldrazi Temple: Provides 2 mana toward Eldrazi spells, enabling turn-2 Thought-Knot Seer.
key_cards_explained.Ancient Tomb: Two colorless mana at the cost of life, powering out early threats and lock pieces.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Eye was banned in Modern but remains legal in Legacy as a 1-of tutor. The deck adapted around Eldrazi Temple as the primary accelerant.

historical_timeline.event: Eldrazi menace begins after Oath of the Gatewatch prints Thought-Knot Seer and Reality Smasher. Eye of Ugin and Eldrazi Temple provide broken mana. Deck immediately tier 1 across multiple formats.
key_changes: Thought-Knot Seer and Reality Smasher as threats
Eye of Ugin and Eldrazi Temple for fast mana
Chalice of the Void to lock out cantrips

historical_timeline.event: Post-Eldrazi winter. Eye of Ugin not banned in Legacy (unlike Modern). Deck settles into tier 2 as players learn to fight it. Various builds: mono-colorless, WE, BE.
Initiative creatures from Commander Legends: Battle for Baldur's Gate add a new dimension. Caves of Chaos Adventurer and similar cards work well with Eldrazi fast mana.
key_changes: Initiative creatures added (various Baldur's Gate cards)
Eldrazi Stompy blends with Initiative strategies

historical_timeline.event: Deck continues as a Chalice-based stompy strategy. Not directly affected by Orcish Bowmasters since its creatures are large.
Steady at 3.1%. Chalice of the Void on one shuts down cantrip-heavy decks, keeping Eldrazi relevant as a meta foil.
key_changes: Sowing Mycospawn from MH3 as additional threat

decks.name: Mystic Forge Combo
decks.description: A colorless artifact combo deck that uses Mystic Forge with Sensei's Divining Top and cost reducers to draw the entire deck, winning with Walking Ballista or Karn, the Great Creator. Combines the prison elements of Stompy with a deterministic combo kill.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the sol land + Chalice shell with Eldrazi but adds a deterministic combo finish. Blue Artifacts is a colored variant with Thought Monitor and Emry.
decks.change_notes: Relatively new archetype that emerged with Mystic Forge + Top. Karn, the Great Creator acts as both a toolbox and a combo piece. The wishboard is heavily metagame dependent.

key_cards_explained.Mystic Forge: With Sensei's Divining Top and a cost reducer, creates an infinite draw loop to find Walking Ballista for the kill.
key_cards_explained.Sensei's Divining Top: Key combo piece that draws a card and goes to the top of library, looping with Mystic Forge.
key_cards_explained.Karn, the Great Creator: Tutors silver bullets from the sideboard and shuts off opponent's artifacts. -2 finds the missing combo piece.
key_cards_explained.Grim Monolith: Fast mana that powers out early Mystic Forge or Karn. Acts as the cost reducer for the combo loop.

historical_timeline.event: Mystic Forge combo deck emerges using Mystic Forge with artifact cost reducers to draw through the deck and generate lethal artifact synergies.
key_changes: Artifact cost reducers (Foundry Inspector, Etherium Sculptor)
Karn, the Great Creator as tutor/lock

historical_timeline.event: Deck refines its combo lines and gains MH3 artifacts. Becomes more consistent and rises in the meta.
Solid tier 2 at 3.0%. A colorless combo deck that punishes fair blue strategies with Chalice of the Void and Trinisphere.

decks.name: Doomsday
decks.description: A combo deck that casts Doomsday to stack a five-card pile that wins the game, typically with Thassa's Oracle. Extremely skill-intensive, requiring knowledge of many different piles for various game states. Thassa's Oracle simplified the win condition significantly from earlier Oracle-less builds.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the UB cantrip + disruption shell with Dimir Tempo but uses it to set up a combo kill rather than tempo pressure. Shares the storm adjacency with TES.
decks.change_notes: Thassa's Oracle revolutionized the deck by simplifying pile construction. The protection suite and draw spells flex based on metagame.

key_cards_explained.Doomsday: The namesake card. For BBB, you stack your library with exactly five cards that win the game, then draw into the pile.
key_cards_explained.Thassa's Oracle: Win condition that wins when it enters the battlefield if your devotion to blue exceeds your library size. After Doomsday, this is trivially achieved.
key_cards_explained.Ideas Unbound: Draws three cards from the Doomsday pile for UU, enabling compact winning piles.
key_cards_explained.Street Wraith: Free cantrip that draws through the Doomsday pile without costing mana.

historical_timeline.event: Doomsday exists as a fringe Legacy combo deck using the namesake card to stack a five-card library and win deterministically. Complex execution keeps it niche.
Thassa's Oracle printed (Theros Beyond Death). Dramatically simplifies Doomsday piles — Oracle is much easier to win with than Laboratory Maniac. Deck viability jumps.
Continues as a strong combo option. Personal Tutor and other cantrips help find Doomsday consistently. Compact combo footprint allows flexible shell.
Up the Beanstalk and other card draw options explored. Deck remains a skill-testing tier 2 combo option.
key_changes: Cavern of Souls for Oracle resolution

historical_timeline.event: Consistent tier 2 at 2.7%. Rewards mastery and punishes opponents who can't interact with the stack.

decks.name: Red Stompy
decks.description: A mono-red prison deck that uses sol lands to deploy Blood Moon, Chalice of the Void, and Trinisphere to lock opponents out of the game while attacking with efficient red threats like Goblin Rabblemaster and Fury. Punishes greedy mana bases.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the sol land + Chalice + Blood Moon shell with Painter but lacks the combo finish, relying on creature damage instead.
decks.change_notes: Caves of Chaos Adventurer from Commander Legends was a significant upgrade. Urza's Saga provides additional value. The threat suite rotates frequently.

key_cards_explained.Blood Moon: Turns all nonbasic lands into Mountains, devastating multi-color decks that rely on fetchlands and duals.
key_cards_explained.Chalice of the Void: On 1, shuts down most of the format's cantrips and interaction. The deck's spells are designed to avoid being hit.
key_cards_explained.Goblin Rabblemaster: Generates tokens every turn and grows itself. Ends games quickly once the opponent is locked under prison pieces.
key_cards_explained.Caves of Chaos Adventurer: Initiative creature that generates value from the undercity, providing card advantage in a prison shell.

historical_timeline.event: Mono-Red Stompy (Dragon Stompy) emerges as a Legacy archetype using Blood Moon, Chalice of the Void, and Chrome Mox to lock opponents out while beating down with aggressive red threats.
key_changes: Blood Moon + Chalice of the Void lock pieces
Chrome Mox, Simian Spirit Guide for fast mana
Ancient Tomb and City of Traitors

historical_timeline.event: Deck remains a budget-friendly way into Legacy. Various threat suites tried including Goblin Rabblemaster.
key_changes: Magus of the Moon as redundancy

historical_timeline.event: Modern Horizons adds Seasoned Pyromancer. Chandra, Torch of Defiance provides card advantage and a win condition.
key_changes: Chandra, Torch of Defiance as planeswalker threat

historical_timeline.event: Fable of the Mirror-Breaker adds powerful three-drop. Initiative creatures (Caves of Chaos Adventurer) give the deck a new powerful angle.
key_changes: Fable of the Mirror-Breaker for value

historical_timeline.event: MH3 energy cards not useful for mono-red. Deck continues with prison + threats strategy.
key_changes: Broadside Bombardiers and other new red threats

historical_timeline.event: Solid at 2.7%. Blood Moon and Chalice remain devastating against greedy mana bases and cantrip-heavy decks.

decks.name: Affinity Stompy
decks.description: A colorless artifact aggro deck that leverages affinity creatures, Urza's Saga constructs, and Cranial Plating to deal massive damage quickly. Plays cheap or free artifact creatures alongside Chalice of the Void to combine aggression with disruption.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the artifact-heavy strategy with Mystic Forge and Blue Artifacts but is more aggressive with affinity creatures and Cranial Plating.
decks.change_notes: Urza's Saga elevated the entire artifact aggro archetype. Modern Horizons printings have steadily improved the creature base.

key_cards_explained.Cranial Plating: Attaches to any creature for lethal damage in a deck full of artifacts. Can be moved at instant speed.
key_cards_explained.Urza's Saga: Tutors for Cranial Plating, Mox Opal, or Springleaf Drum while creating Construct tokens that scale with artifact count.
key_cards_explained.Thought Monitor: Affinity for artifacts makes this a 1-2 mana 2/2 flyer that draws 2 cards.
key_cards_explained.Mox Opal: Free mana that enables explosive starts and contributes to affinity counts.

historical_timeline.event: Modern artifact cards and new printings make an artifact aggro deck viable in Legacy. Thought Monitor, Sojourner's Companion, and Nettlecyst create a critical mass of artifact synergies.
Deck refined with MH3 artifacts. Cranial Ram and other new printings add power. Chalice of the Void gives it disruption.
key_changes: Chalice of the Void for disruption
Frogmite and Myr Enforcer as cheap threats

historical_timeline.event: Established tier 2 at 2.4%. A fast artifact aggro deck with Chalice disruption that can race combo and go under control.

decks.name: Beanstalk Control
decks.description: A four-color control deck built around Up the Beanstalk as a card advantage engine. Playing 5+ mana value cards like Force of Will, Leyline Binding, and Solitude for free triggers Beanstalk's draw ability. Grinds opponents out with removal and card advantage.
decks.similarity_notes: Jeskai Control is a leaner three-color version that trades Beanstalk card advantage for more countermagic. Both are blue control decks with white removal.
decks.change_notes: The exact color combination and threat/removal suite shifts frequently. Some builds lean Jeskai, others splash black or green more heavily. Up the Beanstalk from Wilds of Eldraine was the catalyst for the archetype.

key_cards_explained.Up the Beanstalk: Draws a card on ETB and whenever you cast a 5+ MV spell. Free-cast evoke creatures and Force of Will all trigger it.
key_cards_explained.Leyline Binding: Six-mana removal spell that costs W with enough domain, triggering Beanstalk while being efficient removal.
key_cards_explained.Solitude: Evoke for free exile removal that triggers Beanstalk. Provides a 3/2 lifelink body when hard-cast.
key_cards_explained.LÃ³rien Revealed: Draws 3 cards for 5 mana (triggering Beanstalk) or island-cycles for 1 to fix mana early.

historical_timeline.event: Up the Beanstalk from Wilds of Eldraine enables 4-color control piles. Drawing a card whenever you cast a 5+ MV spell (including Force of Will, Solitude, etc.) provides incredible card advantage.
key_changes: Up the Beanstalk as card engine

historical_timeline.event: Deck establishes itself as the premier control strategy. Various builds from Jeskai to 4-color. Competes with Miracles-style UW as the control deck of choice.
key_changes: Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath in some builds

historical_timeline.event: Steady at 2.3%. The format's primary multicolor control strategy, though vulnerable to Wasteland-heavy decks and fast combo.

decks.name: Cradle Control
decks.description: A green-based midrange deck that leverages Gaea's Cradle to generate enormous amounts of mana, deploying powerful creatures and planeswalkers. Uses Green Sun's Zenith as a creature toolbox. Combines mana acceleration with disruptive elements like Collector Ouphe.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Green Sun's Zenith toolbox and Gaea's Cradle engine with Elves but is slower and more controlling. Maverick is a more creature-focused GW approach.
decks.change_notes: Once Upon a Time provided significant consistency. The Depths combo package is a flex slot some builds omit for more controlling options.

key_cards_explained.Gaea's Cradle: Taps for one green mana per creature you control. With even 3-4 creatures, provides enormous mana advantage.
key_cards_explained.Green Sun's Zenith: Creature toolbox that finds the right answer for any situation, from Dryad Arbor on turn 1 to Craterhoof for the kill.
key_cards_explained.Collector Ouphe: Shuts down all activated abilities of artifacts, devastating against Urza's Saga, Mox Opal, and artifact combo decks.
key_cards_explained.Titania, Protector of Argoth: Creates 5/3 tokens whenever a land goes to the graveyard, synergizing with Wasteland and fetchlands.

historical_timeline.event: Green stompy/toolbox deck built around Gaea's Cradle and Green Sun's Zenith emerges. Uses Elvish Reclaimer, Collector Ouphe, and other green creatures with Cradle for explosive mana.
MH3 energy creatures (Ocelot Pride, etc.) can splash into Cradle builds. Some overlap with Ocelot Pride Midrange but Cradle Control focuses on the mana engine.
Tier 2 at 2.3%. A Gaea's Cradle-powered creature deck that goes over the top with mana advantage.

decks.name: Death and Taxes
decks.description: A white-based creature deck that disrupts opponents with Thalia, Guardian of Thraben, Wasteland + Rishadan Port mana denial, and Aether Vial to deploy threats at instant speed. The Yorion 80-card version with a black splash has become standard, providing more consistency in grindy matchups.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the white creature-based disruption plan with Maverick. Ocelot Pride Midrange is the newer energy-based competitor for the non-blue aggro slot.
decks.change_notes: The shift to 80-card Yorion with black splash for Orcish Bowmasters and Thoughtseize was the biggest change. The deck constantly adapts its creature suite to the metagame.

key_cards_explained.Thalia, Guardian of Thraben: Taxes noncreature spells by 1, slowing down cantrip and combo decks significantly.
key_cards_explained.Aether Vial: Deploys creatures at instant speed without paying mana, circumventing Thalia's own tax and enabling tricks with Flickerwisp.
key_cards_explained.Stoneforge Mystic: Tutors and cheats equipment into play. Kaldra Compleat or Batterskull provides a devastating threat.
key_cards_explained.Yorion, Sky Nomad: Companion that blinks permanents for value. Enables the 80-card build with extra silver bullets.
key_cards_explained.Orcish Bowmasters: The black splash payoff. Punishes cantrips and provides removal.

historical_timeline.event: Death and Taxes becomes a Legacy staple as a mono-white hatebear deck. Thalia, Guardian of Thraben from Dark Ascension is the signature card, taxing the cantrip-heavy format. Aether Vial provides tempo.
key_changes: Thalia, Guardian of Thraben as format-defining hatebear

historical_timeline.event: Consistent tier 2 presence. Spirit of the Labyrinth from Born of the Gods added as Brainstorm hoser.
key_changes: Recruiter of the Guard for toolbox access

historical_timeline.event: Top ban guts Miracles, and D&T benefits from less Terminus in the format. Rises in meta share as one of the best fair decks.
key_changes: Less Terminus means creatures survive better

historical_timeline.event: Once Upon a Time briefly legal. Palace Jailer provides card advantage. Steady performer through multiple metagame shifts.
key_changes: Gideon, Ally of Zendikar in some builds

historical_timeline.event: Yorion, Sky Nomad companion explored in 80-card builds. Skyclave Apparition adds flexible removal.
Orcish Bowmasters pushes some builds toward BW (Orzhov). Bowmasters is excellent in the shell, and Plague Engineer was already played. Grief adds disruption.
Tier 2 at 2.0%. Adapts to every meta but never dominates. One of Legacy's most beloved and enduring fair decks.
key_changes: Both mono-W and BW builds viable

decks.name: Omni-Tell
decks.description: A combo deck that uses Show and Tell to put Omniscience into play, then casts free spells to win the game with Cunning Wish into Release the Ants or simply casting Emrakul. More resilient than Sneak and Show against creature removal but more vulnerable to enchantment removal.
decks.similarity_notes: Both use Show and Tell as the combo enabler but diverge on the payoff: Omniscience vs Sneak Attack. Omni-Tell is more all-in on the enchantment plan.
decks.change_notes: Very stable combo. The Cunning Wish targets in the sideboard rotate based on metagame. Veil of Summer is a key recent addition for protection.

key_cards_explained.Omniscience: Allows casting all spells for free. Once in play, chain Cunning Wish into the winning combo or just cast Emrakul.
key_cards_explained.Cunning Wish: With Omniscience in play, tutors for the win condition from the sideboard.
key_cards_explained.Release the Ants: Win condition with Omniscience: clash repeatedly, always winning since Emrakul has the highest MV, dealing infinite damage.

historical_timeline.event: Omni-Tell splits from Sneak and Show, focusing on Show and Tell into Omniscience rather than Sneak Attack. Casts Emrakul for free or uses Cunning Wish to chain spells.
key_changes: Omniscience as primary Show and Tell target
Release the Ants / Emrakul as win conditions

historical_timeline.event: Dig Through Time briefly makes Omni-Tell insane before being banned. The deck could consistently find combo pieces. Remains viable after the ban.
key_changes: Dig Through Time briefly incredible (then banned)

historical_timeline.event: Thassa's Oracle win condition simplifies some builds. Omniscience + Oracle + cantrips is cleaner than older wins.
key_changes: Thassa's Oracle as cleaner win condition

historical_timeline.event: Tier 2 at 1.7%. Exists alongside Sneak and Show as the slower but more resilient Show and Tell variant.
key_changes: Competes with Sneak and Show for players

decks.name: Mono Black Midrange
decks.description: A mono-black midrange deck leveraging the power of Orcish Bowmasters, Sheoldred, the Apocalypse, and discard-based disruption. Eschews blue cantrips for a more consistent mana base and powerful black staples. Uses Urza's Saga and Dark Ritual for acceleration.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the black disruption core of Dimir Tempo but trades blue cantrips for more raw power and a simpler mana base. A more midrange approach than Dimir Tempo's tempo plan.
decks.change_notes: Bowmasters and Sheoldred made mono-black viable. The reanimation package (Grief + Reanimate) gives explosive starts. Still a relatively new archetype being refined.

key_cards_explained.Grief: Evoked on turn 1 with Reanimate provides a discard effect plus a 3/2 menace body, recreating the Scam strategy.
key_cards_explained.Sheoldred, the Apocalypse: Gains life when you draw and drains opponent when they draw. Punishes blue cantrips mercilessly.
key_cards_explained.Hymn to Tourach: Random discard of two cards for BB. One of the most powerful disruption spells in the format.
key_cards_explained.Reanimate: Brings back Grief, Archon of Cruelty, or stolen creatures for just 1 mana.

historical_timeline.event: Mono-Black Midrange / Scam decks emerge using Grief + Reanimate for turn-1 disruption alongside Orcish Bowmasters and Sheoldred, the Apocalypse.
Necrodominance from MH3 provides a Necropotence-like card advantage engine. Some builds incorporate it.
Tier 2 at 1.3%. A pure black disruption deck that leverages the best black cards in the format.
key_changes: Tourach, Dread Cantor and other black threats

decks.name: Aluren
decks.description: A combo deck built around the enchantment Aluren, which allows creatures with MV 3 or less to be cast for free at instant speed. Combines with Acererak the Archlich to repeatedly venture into the dungeon, draining the opponent's life to zero with Tomb of Annihilation.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the BUG shell and some creature overlap with Sultai Tempo. The GSZ package is similar to Cradle Control but aimed at finding combo pieces.
decks.change_notes: Acererak the Archlich from AFR was a transformative addition, providing a compact infinite combo with Aluren. Previous versions used more complex loops with Cavern Harpy.

key_cards_explained.Aluren: Enchantment that lets both players cast creatures with MV 3 or less for free. Enables the combo.
key_cards_explained.Acererak the Archlich: With Aluren, cast for free, ventures into dungeon, bounces to hand if you haven't completed Tomb of Annihilation. Repeat for infinite drain.
key_cards_explained.Ice-Fang Coatl: Flash deathtouch creature that draws a card. Excellent with Aluren and provides interaction.
key_cards_explained.Cavern of Souls: Names Zombie for Acererak, ensuring the combo piece resolves through countermagic.

historical_timeline.event: Aluren combo deck exists in early Legacy, using the enchantment Aluren to cast creatures for free and generate infinite loops with Cavern Harpy.
key_changes: Parasitic Strix / Dream Stalker for win

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman era. BUG Aluren lists use DRS and other value creatures alongside the combo. True-Name Nemesis gives a fair plan B.
DRS ban hurts the deck's fair game plan. Aluren adapts but loses a key mana creature.
Orcish Bowmasters can be cast for free off Aluren, adding removal to the creature combo shell. Deck gets a resurgence.
Tier 2 at 1.2%. A unique creature combo deck with a loyal player base and the ability to play a fair game.

decks.name: Blue Artifacts
decks.description: A blue-black artifact deck that combines Thought Monitor, Emry, Lurker of the Loch, and Urza's Saga to create an artifact-synergy midrange strategy backed by blue countermagic. More interactive than pure Affinity builds.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the artifact creature base with Affinity Stompy but adds blue interaction. Less combo-focused than Mystic Forge but more resilient.
decks.change_notes: Relatively new archetype combining the best of artifact aggro with blue interaction. Emry provides recursion that pure Affinity lacks.

key_cards_explained.Emry, Lurker of the Loch: Costs as little as U with enough artifacts. Recurs artifacts from the graveyard each turn, providing incredible value.
key_cards_explained.Thought Monitor: Draws two cards for as little as U when you have enough artifacts. The deck's premier card advantage.
key_cards_explained.Mox Opal: Free mana that enables explosive starts and is recurrable with Emry.

historical_timeline.event: Blue-based artifact combo-control deck emerges using Emry, Lurker of the Loch, Urza's Saga, and various artifact synergies. Blue provides Force of Will backup.
key_changes: Emry, Lurker of the Loch as engine
Urza's Saga for tutoring and threats

historical_timeline.event: MH3 artifact support helps the deck. Refines into a more consistent strategy.
Tier 2 at 1.1%. A niche artifact strategy that leverages blue permission alongside artifact synergies.

decks.name: LED Dredge
decks.description: A graveyard combo deck that uses the dredge mechanic to fill its graveyard, then wins with Narcomoeba, Bridge from Below, and Dread Return into a win condition. Lion's Eye Diamond enables explosive starts by providing mana while filling the graveyard.
decks.similarity_notes: Both are graveyard combo decks using Narcomoeba and Dread Return, but Dredge is more resilient and grindy while Oops is faster and more fragile.
decks.change_notes: Very stable core. Silversmote Ghoul was a meaningful addition. Thassa's Oracle provides a backup win condition. The deck adapts primarily through its sideboard.

key_cards_explained.Golgari Grave-Troll: Dredge 6 is the highest dredge count, milling six cards each time you would draw. Fills the graveyard rapidly.
key_cards_explained.Bridge from Below: Creates zombie tokens whenever a creature you control dies, provided it's in your graveyard. Powers the Dread Return chain.
key_cards_explained.Lion's Eye Diamond: Discarding your hand feeds dredge cards into the graveyard while providing 3 mana for Faithless Looting flashback.
key_cards_explained.Ichorid: Recurs from the graveyard each turn for free, providing sacrifice fodder and triggering Bridge from Below.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost a free cantrip that helped enable turn-1 kills. Reduced consistency slightly.

historical_timeline.event: LED Dredge establishes itself as Legacy's premier graveyard deck. Uses Lion's Eye Diamond to enable dredge cards, Narcomoeba, Ichorid, and Bridge from Below for an army of zombies.
key_changes: Golgari Grave-Troll, Stinkweed Imp as dredgers
Narcomoeba, Ichorid, Bridge from Below as payoffs

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman everywhere is devastating for Dredge. Incidental graveyard hate on a maindeckable creature crushes the strategy.
key_changes: Deathrite Shaman makes graveyard strategies much worse

historical_timeline.event: DRS ban is hugely positive for Dredge. Creeping Chill from Guilds of Ravnica adds free damage from the graveyard. Deck surges.
Endurance from MH2 is a devastating hate card — instant-speed graveyard shuffle. Deck takes a hit.
key_changes: Endurance as major new hate card

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters incidentally punishes some Dredge plays. Surgical Extraction and other hate remain widespread.
Tier 2 at 1.1%. Graveyard hate keeps it in check, but the raw power of dredging remains formidable against unprepared opponents.
key_changes: Adapts threat suite to current hate

decks.name: Jeskai Control
decks.description: A traditional three-color control deck using countermagic, removal, and card advantage to grind opponents out. The spiritual successor to Miracles after the Sensei's Divining Top ban, now using Monastery Mentor or Entreat the Angels as win conditions alongside efficient Jeskai interaction.
decks.similarity_notes: Beanstalk Control is the four-color evolution of this strategy. Jeskai Control is leaner and more focused on countermagic over Beanstalk card advantage.
decks.change_notes: Has been somewhat eclipsed by Beanstalk Control as the control deck of choice. Remains viable for players who prefer a tighter mana base.

key_cards_explained.Monastery Mentor: Creates monk tokens on every noncreature spell, turning cantrips into a lethal army.
key_cards_explained.Terminus: Miracle for W puts all creatures on the bottom of libraries. Devastating board wipe that can be set up with Brainstorm.
key_cards_explained.Snapcaster Mage: Recurs any instant or sorcery from the graveyard, providing flexibility and card advantage.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Ended the Miracles era. The deck evolved from Counterbalance/Top lock to a more traditional control build.

historical_timeline.event: After Sensei's Divining Top ban guts Miracles, UW/Jeskai Control rebuilds without the Counterbalance-Top lock. Slower but more traditional control approach with Snapcaster Mage, Jace, and Supreme Verdict.
key_changes: Rebuilds from Miracles shell post-Top ban
Snapcaster Mage + Jace, the Mind Sculptor
Search for Azcanta as card advantage

historical_timeline.event: Shark Typhoon provides a flexible win condition. Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath in Bant or 4c versions. Jeskai with Pyroblast preferred by some.
key_changes: Shark Typhoon as uncounterable win condition

historical_timeline.event: Competes with Beanstalk Control for the control slot. Jeskai offers Pyroblast and Lightning Bolt but Beanstalk offers more raw card advantage.
Tier 3 at 0.9%. Still viable but overshadowed by Beanstalk Control as the control deck of choice.

decks.name: Selesnya Depths
decks.description: A combo deck focused on assembling Dark Depths + Thespian's Stage as quickly as possible using Crop Rotation, Elvish Reclaimer, and Knight of the Reliquary. The GW version adds Swords to Plowshares and Knight toolbox over the older BG version.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Depths combo with Lands but in a more aggressive creature-based shell. Shares the GW creature toolbox with Maverick.
decks.change_notes: The shift from BG to GW happened gradually as Swords to Plowshares and Knight of the Reliquary proved more impactful. Once Upon a Time added consistency.

key_cards_explained.Dark Depths: Combines with Thespian's Stage for a 20/20 flying indestructible token as early as turn 2.
key_cards_explained.Crop Rotation: Instant-speed land tutor that finds Dark Depths or Thespian's Stage at the cost of sacrificing a land.
key_cards_explained.Knight of the Reliquary: Tutors lands including combo pieces. Grows larger as lands enter the graveyard from fetches and sacrifices.
key_cards_explained.Sejiri Steppe: Fetched by Knight to give Marit Lage protection from a color, ensuring the 20/20 connects.

historical_timeline.event: GW Depths focuses on the Dark Depths + Thespian's Stage combo with Knight of the Reliquary as a land tutor and backup threat. Green Sun's Zenith toolbox.
key_changes: Knight of the Reliquary as tutor/threat

historical_timeline.event: DRS ban helps the deck. Ramunap Excavator provides additional land recursion and Wasteland synergy.
key_changes: DRS ban removes hate and competition

historical_timeline.event: Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth from MH2 makes the Depths combo easier to assemble — all lands become Forests for Crop Rotation.
key_changes: Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth enables easier combo

historical_timeline.event: Competes with Lands for the land-combo slot. GW Depths is faster but less grindy.
Tier 3 at 0.8%. A focused Dark Depths combo deck that rewards knowledge of the format's mana bases.
key_changes: Occasional Abrupt Decay splash for BG Depths

decks.name: Sultai Tempo
decks.description: A three-color tempo deck that adds green to the UB shell for Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath and Endurance. Slightly grindier than Dimir Tempo with better long-game potential but a less consistent mana base.
decks.similarity_notes: Essentially Dimir Tempo with a green splash. Trades mana consistency for Uro's grinding power and Endurance as graveyard hate.
decks.change_notes: Has been largely supplanted by straight Dimir Tempo, which has a cleaner mana base. Green splash is only worthwhile if Uro and Endurance are particularly well positioned.

key_cards_explained.Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath: The primary reason to splash green. Provides life, card draw, and ramp, then escapes as a 6/6 that does it all again.
key_cards_explained.Endurance: Flash creature that shuffles graveyards, providing hate against Dredge, Reanimator, and Doomsday.
key_cards_explained.Abrupt Decay: Uncounterable removal for permanents CMC 3 or less. Hits Chalice of the Void, Blood Moon, and most threats.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost one of the best reasons to play green in a tempo shell. Contributed to the decline of Sultai builds.

historical_timeline.event: BUG Delver rises to tier 1 with Deathrite Shaman. Combines Delver, DRS, Tarmogoyf, True-Name Nemesis with Abrupt Decay, Force of Will, and Hymn to Tourach. The best deck in Legacy for several years.
key_changes: Deathrite Shaman as mana creature + graveyard hate + win condition

historical_timeline.event: BUG Delver remains one of the two best decks alongside Miracles. DRS + Wasteland + Daze is the defining tempo shell.
key_changes: Leovold, Emissary of Trest added (then banned 2017)

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman BANNED (July). BUG Delver completely collapses without its best card. Players scatter to RUG Delver and other shells.
key_changes: Most players switch to other Delver variants

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters gives black a reason to exist in tempo again, but Dimir Tempo (UB) is preferred over BUG (adding green for Uro).
key_changes: UB Dimir Tempo preferred over BUG
Some BUG lists with Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath

historical_timeline.event: Tier 3 at 0.5%. Niche option for players who want Uro alongside the UB tempo shell.

decks.name: Death's Shadow
decks.description: An aggressive tempo deck built around Death's Shadow as a massive undercosted threat. Uses fetchlands, Watery Grave, and other life-payment cards to reduce its own life total, making Shadow a 1-mana 8/8 or larger. Combines with the standard blue-black disruption package.
decks.similarity_notes: Very close to Dimir Tempo but trades some consistency for the explosive power of Death's Shadow. The life-payment theme makes the deck more aggressive but riskier.
decks.change_notes: Has been largely absorbed into the broader Dimir Tempo category. Players who run Shadow tend to be specialists. Reanimate adds a secondary plan of rebuying large threats.

key_cards_explained.Death's Shadow: Gets bigger as your life total gets lower. At 7 life, it's a 1-mana 6/6. Fetchlands and Thoughtseize naturally lower your life.
key_cards_explained.Snuff Out: Free removal that costs 4 life, which is actually a benefit since it grows Death's Shadow.
key_cards_explained.Street Wraith: Cycles for 2 life, replacing itself while shrinking your life total for Shadow.
key_cards_explained.Reanimate: Brings back Shadow or Bowmasters from the graveyard while paying life to fuel Shadow.

historical_timeline.event: Death's Shadow decks appear in Legacy, translating from Modern. Uses the format's free spells, fetchlands, and Thoughtseize to fuel a cheap, massive threat.
key_changes: Thoughtseize + fetchlands + Force for life payment

historical_timeline.event: MH2 gives Dragon's Rage Channeler and Murktide Regent, which fit perfectly in the same shell. Grixis Death's Shadow gains power.
Bowmasters in Dimir Shadow is excellent. The UB shell is essentially Dimir Tempo with Death's Shadow as the payoff threat.
Tier 3 at 0.5%. A more aggressive variant of the Dimir Tempo shell for players who like the Shadow playstyle.
key_changes: More aggressive than standard Dimir Tempo

decks.name: Infect
decks.description: An aggressive combo-aggro deck that wins by dealing 10 poison counters using infect creatures pumped with free spells like Invigorate and Berserk. Can win as early as turn 2 and rarely later than turn 4 if uninterrupted.
decks.similarity_notes: Both are green creature-based aggro decks that can win quickly. Infect is more combo-oriented while Elves is more synergy-driven.
decks.change_notes: Scale Up from MH1 was the last major addition. The deck is extremely stable but struggles against the prevalence of Orcish Bowmasters.

key_cards_explained.Invigorate: Free +4/+4 that enables turn-2 kills. The life gain to the opponent doesn't matter when you win with poison.
key_cards_explained.Berserk: Doubles a creature's power for G. Combined with infect, this creates lethal damage on tiny investments.
key_cards_explained.Blighted Agent: Unblockable infect creature. With any pump spell, represents a fast clock that can't be blocked.
key_cards_explained.Vines of Vastwood: Dual-purpose card that protects creatures from removal and pumps them for +4/+4 when kicked.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost perfect information before going all-in on the combo turn. Reduced the deck's ability to play around removal.

historical_timeline.event: Infect emerges in Legacy with Glistener Elf, Blighted Agent, and Invigorate (free pump spell). Can kill on turn 2. Uses Brainstorm, Force of Will, and Daze for protection.
key_changes: Glistener Elf and Blighted Agent as infect threats

historical_timeline.event: DRS era is tough for Infect as the format has more incidental interaction. Deck remains viable but not dominant.
key_changes: Deck remains fast enough to compete

historical_timeline.event: DRS ban removes a common roadblock. Gitaxian Probe ban hurts information gathering. Mixed impact.
MH2 threats make other decks more efficient. Infect struggles against Murktide Regent and Dragon's Rage Channeler.
key_changes: Deck speed still relevant vs combo

historical_timeline.event: Tier 3 at 0.4%. A glass cannon that can steal games but struggles in the Bowmasters era.

decks.name: Burn
decks.description: The oldest archetype in Magic. Aims to deal 20 damage to the opponent using direct damage spells as quickly as possible. Budget-friendly and straightforward but struggles against the format's efficient answers and life gain.
decks.similarity_notes: Both are mono-red but take very different approaches. Burn goes face while Red Stompy locks the opponent out with prison pieces.
decks.change_notes: Extremely stable. Horizon lands (Fiery Islet, Sunbaked Canyon) were the last meaningful additions. The deck's power level hasn't kept pace with format upgrades.

key_cards_explained.Price of Progress: Deals 2 damage per nonbasic land the opponent controls. In Legacy, most decks play 12+ nonbasics, making this 6-10 damage for 1R.
key_cards_explained.Eidolon of the Great Revel: Deals 2 damage to any player who casts a spell with MV 3 or less. In Legacy, nearly every spell triggers this.
key_cards_explained.Fireblast: Free 4 damage by sacrificing two Mountains. The deck's finisher that lets you tap out for other spells then Fireblast for lethal.

historical_timeline.event: Burn has existed since Magic's earliest days. Mountains and Lightning Bolts. The simplest and most direct strategy in the game.
key_changes: Price of Progress punishes nonbasic lands

historical_timeline.event: Modern Legacy format. Burn is a budget-friendly entry point. Goblin Guide from Zendikar is a major addition.
key_changes: Eidolon of the Great Revel added later (2014)

historical_timeline.event: Eidolon of the Great Revel from Journey into Nyx is perfect for Legacy where most spells cost 1-2 mana. Punishes cantrip-heavy format.
key_changes: Eidolon of the Great Revel as format punisher

historical_timeline.event: MH2 power creep makes Burn less competitive. Ragavan and Murktide decks are too efficient.
key_changes: Still cheap entry point to Legacy

historical_timeline.event: Orcish Bowmasters kills Goblin Guide and Eidolon. Burn takes a significant hit from the most-played creature in the format.
Fringe at 0.3%. Still exists as an ultra-budget entry to Legacy but not competitively viable against modern threats.
key_changes: Fundamentally outclassed by format power level

decks.name: Elves
decks.description: A creature-based combo deck that uses elf tribal synergies and Gaea's Cradle to generate massive amounts of mana, then wins with Craterhoof Behemoth or Natural Order. Once a format staple, now pushed to the fringes by Orcish Bowmasters which devastates the small creature strategy.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares Gaea's Cradle and Green Sun's Zenith with Cradle Control but is faster and more all-in on the creature combo. Many Elves players have moved to Cradle Control.
decks.change_notes: Orcish Bowmasters has been devastating for this archetype. A single Bowmasters trigger off Glimpse of Nature can wipe the board. The deck persists through dedicated pilots but is far from its former glory.

key_cards_explained.Glimpse of Nature: Draws a card for each creature cast in a turn. With Heritage Druid mana, chains through the deck to find Craterhoof.
key_cards_explained.Heritage Druid: Taps three elves for GGG, even ones with summoning sickness. Enables explosive turns with multiple cheap elves.
key_cards_explained.Natural Order: Sacrifices an elf to put Craterhoof Behemoth directly into play, often winning on the spot.
key_cards_explained.Gaea's Cradle: With 5+ elves in play, generates enough mana for virtually anything.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost a key one-drop that provided mana fixing and utility. Significantly weakened the deck.

historical_timeline.event: Elves combo deck emerges using Glimpse of Nature, Heritage Druid, and Nettle Sentinel to generate massive mana and cards, finishing with Craterhoof Behemoth or Natural Order.
key_changes: Glimpse of Nature as draw engine
Heritage Druid + Nettle Sentinel for mana

historical_timeline.event: Elves remains competitive but Miracles and Terminus are devastating — sweeping the board of small creatures repeatedly.
key_changes: Terminus from Miracles is a nightmare
Deathrite Shaman added to BG builds

historical_timeline.event: Top ban weakens Miracles, which is great for creature decks like Elves. Brief resurgence.
DRS ban removes a key creature from Elves builds that splashed black. Deck refocuses on mono-green.
Orcish Bowmasters is catastrophic for Elves. Kills mana dorks and punishes Glimpse of Nature draws. Deck drops to fringe.
key_changes: Bowmasters kills every creature in the deck
Deck nearly unplayable in Bowmasters meta

historical_timeline.event: Fringe at 0.2%. Bowmasters has essentially killed the archetype. Elves players have largely migrated to Cradle Control or Ocelot Pride Midrange.
key_changes: Players migrated to other green decks

decks.name: Maverick
decks.description: A GW creature toolbox deck using Green Sun's Zenith, Knight of the Reliquary, and mana denial via Wasteland and Thalia. Once a premier fair deck, now largely supplanted by Ocelot Pride Midrange and Death and Taxes as the non-blue creature strategy of choice.
decks.similarity_notes: Ocelot Pride Midrange has largely replaced Maverick as the non-blue creature deck of choice. Both use Green Sun's Zenith and Swords to Plowshares in a GW shell.
decks.change_notes: The archetype has been in decline as newer creature strategies (Energy, Death and Taxes with Yorion) have emerged. Dedicated pilots keep it alive but it lacks a unique edge.

key_cards_explained.Knight of the Reliquary: The deck's signature threat. Grows with each land in the graveyard and tutors key utility lands.
key_cards_explained.Green Sun's Zenith: Versatile creature tutor that provides the right answer for any situation.
key_cards_explained.Thalia, Guardian of Thraben: Taxes noncreature spells, slowing down combo and control while you attack with creatures.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Lost the best one-drop in the format that provided mana fixing, life gain, and graveyard hate. Major blow to the archetype.
Wrenn and Six initially made the Punishing Fire version stronger but was banned due to its impact on the broader format.

historical_timeline.event: Maverick emerges as a GW(b) creature-based midrange deck using Knight of the Reliquary, Green Sun's Zenith, and Stoneforge Mystic. A non-blue fair deck that attacks mana with Wasteland and Thalia.
key_changes: Knight of the Reliquary as cornerstone threat

historical_timeline.event: Deathrite Shaman gives Abzan/Junk Maverick a powerful mana creature. Deck competes with BUG Delver for the green fair deck slot.
DRS ban hurts Maverick but less than BUG Delver. Knight of the Reliquary remains powerful. Deck continues as a solid tier 2 option.
key_changes: DRS ban is a mild negative

historical_timeline.event: MH2 power creep pushes creature decks down. Ragavan and Murktide outclass Maverick's threats.
Many Maverick players migrate to Ocelot Pride Midrange, which uses a similar GSZ + creatures shell but with more powerful energy threats.
key_changes: Players migrate to Ocelot Pride Midrange
Energy creatures outclass old Maverick threats

historical_timeline.event: Fringe at 0.2%. Mostly replaced by Ocelot Pride Midrange, which does the GW creature strategy better.
key_changes: Functionally replaced by Ocelot Pride Midrange

decks.name: Reanimator
decks.description: A fast combo deck that puts massive creatures into the graveyard and reanimates them with spells like Reanimate and Animate Dead. Post-Entomb ban in November 2025, the deck has adapted to use Faithless Looting and Grief as discard outlets but lost significant consistency. Still capable of turn-one kills via Dark Ritual into Reanimate.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the fast combo role with Oops! All Spells but is more resilient and interactive. Both aim to cheat large creatures into play quickly.
decks.change_notes: The Entomb ban in November 2025 was devastating, removing the deck's best enabler. Lists have shifted to Faithless Looting and increased Grief counts. The Show and Tell sideboard plan has become more important.

key_cards_explained.Reanimate: One mana to return any creature from any graveyard to play. The life loss is irrelevant when reanimating Griselbrand or Archon of Cruelty.
key_cards_explained.Grief: Free discard outlet that also strips the opponent's hand. Can be reanimated for value or evoked and immediately targeted with Reanimate.
key_cards_explained.Archon of Cruelty: The primary reanimation target. Draws a card, forces a discard, removes a permanent, drains 3 life.
key_cards_explained.Dark Ritual: Enables turn-one Reanimate plays by providing BBB for one mana.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Removed the deck's primary enabler. Entomb was a one-mana instant-speed tutor to graveyard, enabling consistent turn-one kills. Deck dropped from tier 1 to tier 2.

historical_timeline.event: Early reanimator strategies appear using Reanimate and Buried Alive.
Modern Legacy format. Reanimator uses Entomb and Careful Study for consistent fast kills.
Archon of Cruelty and later printings improve the reanimation targets.
Grief from MH2 becomes key. Grief + Reanimate is a powerful turn-one play.
Entomb banned November 2025. Deck drops from tier 1 to tier 2. Adapts with Faithless Looting.
Stabilized at 2% meta share post-ban. Still capable of explosive starts but less consistent.

decks.name: Stoneblade
decks.description: A fair blue-white midrange deck built around Stoneforge Mystic fetching Batterskull and Kaldra Compleat. Combines blue card selection and countermagic with efficient white threats and equipment.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the blue-white shell with Jeskai Control but is more proactive with the Stoneforge Mystic package.
decks.change_notes: Equipment targets rotate. Kaldra Compleat replaced Umezawa's Jitte. Teferi, Time Raveler is a recent addition. Splash colors (Esper, Jeskai) are common.

key_cards_explained.Stoneforge Mystic: Tutors for any equipment on ETB and can cheat it into play for 1W.
key_cards_explained.Batterskull: Living weapon with vigilance and lifelink. Nearly unbeatable for aggro decks when cheated in on turn 3.
key_cards_explained.Kaldra Compleat: Indestructible living weapon with first strike and trample.
key_cards_explained.Teferi, Time Raveler: Shuts down opposing instants, bounces problematic permanents, and draws cards.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Banned in Standard but has always been legal in Legacy. The card is the deck's entire identity.

historical_timeline.event: Stoneforge Mystic printed in Worldwake. UW Stoneblade emerges as a premier fair deck.
True-Name Nemesis from Commander 2013 makes Stoneblade even better.
DRS ban reshuffles the fair deck hierarchy.
Bowmasters and new printings push Stoneblade down.
key_changes: Dimir Tempo supplants fair midrange role

historical_timeline.event: Tier 3 at 2%. Loyal pilots keep the deck alive.

decks.name: Pox
decks.description: A symmetrical resource denial deck that uses Smallpox, Hymn to Tourach, and Liliana of the Veil to strip the opponent of lands, cards, and creatures. Wins by breaking the symmetry through Mishra's Factory and planeswalkers.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares resource denial with Death and Taxes but uses black disruption instead of white hate bears.
decks.change_notes: Very stable core. Bowmasters was a significant addition in 2023. Karn wishboard in some builds.

key_cards_explained.Smallpox: Each player discards, sacrifices a creature, sacrifices a land, and loses 1 life. Devastating when you break the symmetry.
key_cards_explained.Hymn to Tourach: Random discard of two cards for BB. One of the most powerful disruption spells in Magic.
key_cards_explained.Liliana of the Veil: Repeatable edict and discard. Grinds opponents out over multiple turns.
key_cards_explained.Nether Spirit: Returns from graveyard during upkeep if it's the only creature there. Perfect symmetry-breaker.

historical_timeline.event: Pox (the card) printed in Ice Age. Resource denial viable from Magic's earliest days.
Smallpox from Time Spiral provides a more efficient version of the strategy.
key_changes: Bloodghast and Nether Spirit for symmetry breaking

historical_timeline.event: Liliana of the Veil from Innistrad is perfect. Deck reaches peak viability.
key_changes: Liliana of the Veil as key planeswalker

historical_timeline.event: Bowmasters gives mono-black a powerful new card.
Tier 3 at 2%. Loyal following and Bowmasters has improved creature matchups.

decks.name: Mardu Energy
decks.description: An aggressive energy-based deck combining Ocelot Pride and Amped Raptor with black disruption and Broadside Bombardiers. Leverages MH3 energy creatures alongside Thoughtseize and efficient removal.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the energy creature core with Ocelot Pride Midrange but adds black for Thoughtseize, Bowmasters, and Broadside Bombardiers.
decks.change_notes: New archetype still evolving rapidly. Some builds lean into the Grief package, others focus on pure energy aggro.

key_cards_explained.Broadside Bombardiers: Menace creature that sacrifices on attack to deal 3 damage and give another attacker +1/+0 and trample.
key_cards_explained.Ocelot Pride: Generates cat tokens when you gain life and deal combat damage.
key_cards_explained.Amped Raptor: Energy creature with cascade equal to energy spent.
key_cards_explained.Thoughtseize: Surgical disruption that protects the aggro plan from sweepers and combo.

historical_timeline.event: MH3 energy cards spark Mardu experimentation. Broadside Bombardiers and Ocelot Pride alongside black disruption.
Deck solidifies as a real contender. Energy aggro with black disruption proves potent against blue.
Tier 2 at 3%. Premier aggressive disruption strategy.

decks.name: Boros Initiative
decks.description: A Chalice of the Void stompy deck that uses fast mana to deploy initiative creatures like Caves of Chaos Adventurer and hate bears like Archon of Emeria.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Chalice + fast mana shell with Red Stompy and Eldrazi. Initiative provides a unique card advantage engine.
decks.change_notes: Initiative creatures are the core. Some builds splash red for Pyroblast and Fable of the Mirror-Breaker.

key_cards_explained.Caves of Chaos Adventurer: 4/4 with initiative for 3W. The Undercity dungeon provides treasure, card selection, damage, and a game-winning effect.
key_cards_explained.Chalice of the Void: On one, shuts down Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords to Plowshares.
key_cards_explained.Archon of Emeria: Restricts opponents to one spell per turn and nonbasic lands enter tapped.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Banned for being too efficient at 3 mana. Deck adapted with Caves of Chaos Adventurer and Seasoned Dungeoneer.

historical_timeline.event: Initiative mechanic from Baldur's Gate arrives. Mono White Stompy immediately adopts the enormously powerful initiative creatures.
White Plume Adventurer banned March. Deck adapts to 4-mana initiative creatures.
Competes with other stompy decks. Boros splash provides Pyroblast access.
key_changes: Competes with Eldrazi and Red Stompy

historical_timeline.event: Tier 2 at 2%. Initiative remains powerful but faces stiff competition.

decks.name: Cloudpost Ramp
decks.description: A big mana deck using Cloudpost and Glimmerpost to generate enormous colorless mana, deploying massive threats like Ugin and Karn Liberated.
decks.similarity_notes: Like Lands, uses nonbasic lands as the engine but goes bigger. Eldrazi shares colorless threats but different mana base.
decks.change_notes: Very stable core. Karn wishboard is the main recent innovation.

key_cards_explained.Cloudpost: Taps for 1 mana for each Locus in play. With four Loci, each taps for 4.
key_cards_explained.Glimmerpost: Second Locus that gains life on ETB.
key_cards_explained.Karn, the Great Creator: Provides wishboard and shuts off opposing artifacts.
key_cards_explained.Ugin, the Spirit Dragon: 8 mana wrath that also exiles. Easy to cast with multiple Cloudposts.

historical_timeline.event: Cloudpost printed in Mirrodin. Locus ramp becomes viable.
Peak popularity with Eldrazi finishers.
Karn, the Great Creator adds wishboard dimension.
Tier 3 at 1%. Niche but powerful when assembling multiple Loci.

decks.name: Hogaak
decks.description: A graveyard-based combo deck using Hogaak, Arisen Necropolis as a free 8/8 trampler, combined with Vengevine and Altar of Dementia for a self-mill win condition.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the graveyard-centric plan with LED Dredge but is creature-based. Both fold to graveyard hate.
decks.change_notes: Core is very stable. Once Upon a Time was a big addition.

key_cards_explained.Hogaak, Arisen Necropolis: Free 8/8 trampler cast by convoking and delving. Recurs from graveyard.
key_cards_explained.Vengevine: Returns from graveyard when you cast two creatures in a turn.
key_cards_explained.Altar of Dementia: Sacrifice outlet that mills. Enables infinite mill with recurring creatures.
key_cards_explained.Stitcher's Supplier: Mills three on ETB and death. Fuels graveyard synergies.

historical_bans_impact.impact: Banned in Modern but legal in Legacy where Force of Will keeps it in check.

historical_timeline.event: Hogaak from MH1 warps Modern. In Legacy, creates a powerful but fair graveyard deck.
Settles into a niche role. Powerful against fair decks but weak to graveyard hate.
key_changes: Altar of Dementia as win condition

historical_timeline.event: Tier 3 at 0.7%. Niche graveyard strategy.

decks.name: Coveted Jewel Combo
decks.description: A combo deck using Coveted Jewel with flicker effects like Flickerwisp and Ephemerate to generate enormous mana and card advantage. The Jewel draws three and adds three on ETB.
decks.similarity_notes: Evolved from Death and Taxes, adding Coveted Jewel combo as a powerful mana engine.
decks.change_notes: Still being optimized. Balance between combo pieces and fair creatures is the key tension.

key_cards_explained.Coveted Jewel: Six mana artifact that draws three and adds three on ETB. Flickering resets both.
key_cards_explained.Ephemerate: One mana instant flicker with rebound. Flickers Jewel twice for six cards.
key_cards_explained.Flickerwisp: 3/1 flyer that exiles something until end of turn. Resets Jewel.
key_cards_explained.Recruiter of the Guard: Tutors for any creature with toughness 2 or less. Finds Flickerwisp.

historical_timeline.event: Coveted Jewel + flicker interaction discovered. Early builds from DnT shells.
Deck refined, puts up tournament results.
Tier 3 at 0.7%. Niche combo-creature hybrid.

decks.name: Ruby Storm
decks.description: A mono-red storm combo deck using Birgi, God of Storytelling and Ruby Medallion to reduce costs, chaining rituals until casting lethal Grapeshot or Empty the Warrens.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the storm goal with TES but uses red rituals and Birgi instead of black tutors and Ad Nauseam.
decks.change_notes: Stable core around Birgi and Ruby Medallion. Bonus Round was a key addition.

key_cards_explained.Birgi, God of Storytelling: Adds R whenever you cast a spell. With Ruby Medallion, every ritual nets extra mana.
key_cards_explained.Bonus Round: Copies every instant and sorcery for the rest of the turn.
key_cards_explained.Burning Wish: Tutors from sideboard for the right finisher.

historical_timeline.event: Birgi from Kaldheim enables mono-red storm.
Refined with Bonus Round. Occasional tournament results.
Tier 3 at 0.4%. Budget storm option, less consistent than TES.

decks.name: Cephalid Breakfast
decks.description: A creature-based combo deck using Cephalid Illusionist with Nomads en-Kor to mill the entire library, winning with Narcomoeba and Dread Return into Thassa's Oracle.
decks.similarity_notes: Like Oops, wins by milling the entire library. Uses a creature combo and can also play a fair Stoneforge Mystic game.
decks.change_notes: Thassa's Oracle replaced Laboratory Maniac. Core combo unchanged since 2003.

key_cards_explained.Cephalid Illusionist: Mills three whenever it becomes the target of a spell or ability.
key_cards_explained.Nomads en-Kor: 0-cost activated ability targets Illusionist unlimited times.
key_cards_explained.Thassa's Oracle: Wins on ETB when library is empty.
key_cards_explained.Aether Vial: Cheats creatures in at instant speed, dodging countermagic.

historical_timeline.event: Cephalid Illusionist + Nomads en-Kor combo discovered.
Known quantity. Aether Vial builds provide fair backup.
Thassa's Oracle from Theros Beyond Death is a massive upgrade.
Tier 3 at 0.4%. Niche combo with fair backup plan.

decks.name: Goblins
decks.description: A tribal aggro deck using Goblin Lackey and Aether Vial to cheat goblins into play. Muxus, Goblin Grandee is the ultimate top-end.
decks.similarity_notes: Shares the Aether Vial tribal approach with Merfolk. Like Elves, a once-dominant creature deck hurt by Bowmasters.
